title: Diagnose corrupted piped output
description: When a tool works at the terminal but breaks in a pipe, isolate stream mixing, TTY assumptions, and buffering.
body: Steps: 1. Reproduce: run `tool | cat` or `tool > file` and compare to terminal output. 2. Redirect streams independently (`tool 2>/dev/null` and `tool 1>/dev/null`) to see what each emits. 3. Check for diagnostics/color leaking into stdout and for code that assumed a TTY. 4. Check buffering: full-buffering can withhold output until exit, breaking interactive pipes. 5. Fix stream routing and TTY detection; flush or line-buffer as needed. Rollback point: before changing buffering mode. Validation: piped output contains only clean data and matches expectations; a tool that only behaves in a terminal but corrupts pipes has a stream-separation or TTY-detection bug.
